steps.title: Identify the list structure
steps.content:
Before the blank we have “the Sonoran Desert, ranked” – a noun phrase whose modifier is set off by a comma.

After the blank we have “and the Ferlo Desert, ranked thirty‑second.” – another noun phrase with a comma‑set off modifier, joined by “and”.

The blank must finish the first item and start the second item in a list where each item contains an internal comma.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon rule
steps.content:
Use a semicolon to separate list items that themselves contain commas.

Choice B provides “fifteenth in size; the Great Salt Desert, ranked thirty‑first;” which puts a semicolon after the first item and after the second item, correctly separating the three items.

The other choices either use commas where semicolons are required, omit needed punctuation, or misplace punctuation, breaking the list structure.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
